tweets.text: "Corley, I think, got it fundamentally wrong in that it said, 'We're going to impose intermediate scrutiny rather than strict scrutiny on this regulation because the underlying thing being regulated isn't actually speech in the pure sense. It's some sort of speech conduct hybrid for a bad purpose, and it's not expressive.' And that has no grounding in past Supreme Court precedent." @valkenburgh on what most First Amendment scholars consider the case on where code loses protections as speech. I agree with Peter. Reminder: the court in Corley was targeting a publisher for linking to a website that allowed users to download the software. They weren't punishing the software users for illegal activity (that was too inconvenient, ofc), they were punishing someone for publishing something.
tweets.summary: "Corley, I think, got it fundamentally wrong in that it said, 'We're going to impose intermediate scrutiny rather than strict scrutiny on this…

tweets.text: . @jessepollak isn't a corporate/securities lawyer so this wasn't maximally precise but people dunking on this don't know what they're talking about either. A debt security that exists solely to get exposure to an equity security is ultimately a rather silly idea that shouldn't be necessary & may have adverse consequences (including ultimate recharacterization by courts and regulators as an equity security). Even if it "works" legally & doesn't get recharacterized, it's ultimately creating more intermediaries, which is contrary to the goals of crypto. And reducing fungibility. I get that people just want "onchain exposure" and resist any quality-control nuance that gets in the way of them celebrating that, but it's worth doing this the right way, reducing intermediation, abstraction, complication, and complexity.
tweets.summary: . @jessepollak isn't a corporate/securities lawyer so this wasn't maximally precise but people dunking on this don't know what they're talking about…

tweets.text: As a crypto lawyer, I watched clients struggle with banking and payments for months (sometimes years). It sucked. That's why I'm grateful to have @altitude as the Presenting Sponsor for @LawofCodeFM. It's the type of product I wish existed five years ago. Nearly every business in emerging tech runs into the same payment problems: 1. multiple entities and counterparties 2. across multiple jurisdictions 3. each needing its own payment infrastructure @SimkinStepan, a lawyer-turned-builder who I interviewed nearly five years ago (Ep. 39), stepped away from his legal career to solve problems for businesses. He's built @altitude as the cure for payment headaches. A command center with one account for stablecoins and fiat, Altitude offers zero-fee transfers, Visa cards with cashback on AI and travel spend, automated bill payments, invoicing ... I could go on. If you have clients with payment headaches, Altitude is worth understanding. Learn more at https://t.co/OPhWPeeAbJ. Thank you to Stepan, @philjacobson and the @altitude team for building a wicked product (and supporting the podcast).

tweets.text: RT @jon_stokes: Look I think I've figured this whole thing out. Follow along as I try to steelman, and tell me where I'm wrong. OpenAI guys on the TL believe that if they can't sell metered inference tokens at a sufficient markup, then they will not have enough of a business to fund the next big training run. They are surely correct about this. They believe that if people release powerful open models, this will probably fatally impact their ability to sell inference tokens at enough of a markup to fund the next big training run. They are surely correct about this, too. They also think that if they cannot fund the next big training run (again, by selling inference tokens at a markup), then NOBODY will be able to fund the next big training run because it means there's no money in it. This last bit seems to me & many others to be not just wrong, but totally bananas in a "guy, have seen the actual software industry and how it works in real life?!" kind of way. There are a lot of ways to monetize software out there in the world. Insofar as inference can add new capabilities to software, there will be lots of ways to monetize it. In other words, if you're telling me, "we can't have a business selling inference if X or Y thing keeps happening," then my only response is, "ok well that sucks for you... sounds like that's a terrible business." But if you're telling me that "selling metered inference tokens is a terrible business" is tantamount to "nobody will fund big training runs that are upstream of more effective & economically valuable inference tokens", then I think you are extremely wrong and should get out more and learn about other parts of the software ecosystem. Workplace automation is huge and will be even bigger in the future as models get better. You can sell workplace automation very profitably in lots of different packages (depending on the workplace and the type of automation). Like, I'm sorry that you really really want to be in the metered inference token business and not the workplace automation business, but them's the breaks. The market wants what the market wants. We all need to live in reality and not beg for Uncle Sam to save us all from open source -- because that was already tried and it didn't work.
tweets.summary: RT @jon_stokes: Look I think I've figured this whole thing out. Follow along as I try to steelman, and tell me where I'm wrong. OpenAI guys on the TL…

tweets.text: "The company that runs Amazon deliveries in Japan is about to start paying a huge chunk of the drivers in yen stablecoins" Jessi on the real world after effect of Japan's new stablecoin law "There has been a major response already. This was a long time coming, so it's not like the switch just flipped, but it has cleared the runway for spot crypto ETFs, the Tokyo Stock Exchange is getting involved" "It's not just trading desk money. What came out today is that the company that runs Amazon deliveries in Japan is about to start paying a huge chunk of the drivers in yen stablecoins" "Which is really showing the real after effect of this law that was just passed"
tweets.summary: "The company that runs Amazon deliveries in Japan is about to start paying a huge chunk of the drivers in yen stablecoins" Jessi on the real world…

tweets.text: Most Bitcoin treasury companies exist to buy more coin, full stop. I asked @LynAldenContact why her new venture, Orange Juice, does the opposite: buy 'boring,' cash-flowing businesses first, then layer a Bitcoin treasury on top at the parent-company level. Could this become the Berkshire Hathaway of Bitcoin?🍊 Timestamps: 🍊 00:56 Lyn Alden lays out the $40M raise behind Orange Juice's core thesis 💵 08:06 Why Lyn Alden targets cash-flowing firms over VC backed startups 📱 11:17 Cape: get 33% off your first six months with code unchained at https://t.co/2ZtLziXsHV 🏦 12:16 How keeping the Bitcoin treasury at the parent level avoids procyclical risk 🪖 25:37 How Orange Juice handles a bleeding company and Ruben Zweiban's Navy SEAL background 📈 32:15 Why Lyn says going public aids liquidity, retail access, and tax deferral ⚠️ 39:15 Why Lyn is critical of Strategy's capital structure and Saylor's kidney line 🗑️ 47:40 Why Lyn views Bitcoin inscriptions as spam despite BIP 110's technical limits ⚛️ 50:47 How Lyn assesses the quantum computing threat to Bitcoin's security

tweets.text: RT @ErikVoorhees: There is a misconception that to enjoy open-source you need to "run your own AI on your own hardware." Hobbyists enjoy such things, 99% of the world doesn't. And the best open source models are vastly larger than what most peoples' hardware can plausibly run. Solution is easy: access any model as a service from https://t.co/HGUdedRs2j (every open and closed model available) Private, unrestricted. Get an API key from Venice, give it to your agent, and you're gtg with no manual setup of anything.

tweets.text: 🚨NEW: @Sen_Alsobrooks tells me the White House’s idea to have the Department of Justice enforce the Clarity Act’s ethics provisions is an “unserious offer” and says she would not support the bill if that is the only enforcement option. Senator @berniemoreno, who has been working with @SenatorLummis to secure an ethics agreement with the White House, told reporters last night that the current proposal would put the DOJ, rather than state attorneys general, in charge of enforcement. “But we’ll keep working from that floor to reach an agreement that holds us all accountable,” Alsobrooks said. Alsobrooks is one of two Democratic senators who voted to advance the Clarity Act out of the Senate Banking Committee in May, and has said her continued support for the bill hinges on strong ethics provisions being included in the final text.
tweets.summary: 🚨NEW: @Sen_Alsobrooks tells me the White House’s idea to have the Department of Justice enforce the Clarity Act’s ethics provisions is an “unserious…

tweets.text: Japan just reclassified crypto as a financial instrument and cut the tax on gains from as high as 55% to a flat 20%.
